From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Disputed territories of India](/w/index.php?title=Disputed_territories_of_India&redirect=no "Disputed territories of India"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/India_disputed_areas_map.svg/250px-India_disputed_areas_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:India_disputed_areas_map.svg)

Map showing disputed territories of India.

There are several **disputed territories of India**. A [territorial dispute](/wiki/Territorial_dispute "Territorial dispute") is a disagreement over the possession or control of land between two or more sovereign states or over the possession or control of land by a new state and [occupying power](/wiki/Occupying_power "Occupying power") after it has conquered the land from a former state no longer currently recognized by the new state.

India faces territorial issues with some of its neighbours – the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The dispute with China also involves the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China") based in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan").[[2]](#cite_note-book-2) India has resolved its un-demarcated border with [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan"), which included multiple irregularities. India also resolved its border disputes with [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka").

## Current disputes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Current disputes")]

### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: China")]

Main article: [Territorial disputes of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Territorial_disputes_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Territorial disputes of the People's Republic of China")

The territorial disputes between India and China result from the [historical consequences of colonialism](/wiki/History_of_colonialism "History of colonialism") in Asia and the lack of clear historical boundary demarcations.[[3]](#cite_note-:Wang2-3): 251 There was one historical attempt to set a proposed boundary, the [McMahon Line](/wiki/McMahon_Line "McMahon Line"), by [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") during the [1913-1914 Simla Convention](/wiki/Simla_Convention "Simla Convention").[[3]](#cite_note-:Wang2-3): 251 The [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)") rejected the proposed boundary.[[3]](#cite_note-:Wang2-3): 251 The unresolved dispute over the boundary became contentious after India gained its independence and the People's Republic of China was established.[[3]](#cite_note-:Wang2-3): 251 The disputed borders are complicated by the lack of administrative presence in the disputed areas, which are remote.[[3]](#cite_note-:Wang2-3): 251

#### Sino-Indian border

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Sino-Indian border")]

Main article: [Line of Actual Control](/wiki/Line_of_Actual_Control "Line of Actual Control")

Two governments claim to be the legitimate Chinese government, the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") (PRC) and the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China") (ROC; commonly called "[Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan")"). They do not recognise the legitimacy of each other. The PRC has actual control of its areas on the India-China border, while the ROC currently has no shared land or maritime border with India. Their views are presented below. The [Line of Actual Control](/wiki/Line_of_Actual_Control "Line of Actual Control") (LAC) is presently the operational border between India and the PRC.

#### People's Republic of China's position

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: People's Republic of China's position")]

Main article: [Sino-Indian border dispute](/wiki/Sino-Indian_border_dispute "Sino-Indian border dispute")

The [Depsang Plains](/wiki/Depsang_Plains "Depsang Plains") are located on the border of the Indian union territory of [Ladakh](/wiki/Ladakh "Ladakh") and the disputed zone of [Aksai Chin](/wiki/Aksai_Chin "Aksai Chin"). The [Chinese Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") controls most of the plains,[[4]](#cite_note-depsangincursion-4) while India controls the western portion of the plains.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The dispute remains unresolved.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[Arunachal Pradesh](/wiki/Arunachal_Pradesh "Arunachal Pradesh") is a state of India created on 20 January 1972, located in the far northeast. It borders the states of [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam") and [Nagaland](/wiki/Nagaland "Nagaland") to the south, and shares international borders with [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma") in the east, [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan") in the west, and China in the north. The majority of the territory is claimed by China, by whom it [South Tibet](/wiki/South_Tibet "South Tibet"). The northern border of Arunachal Pradesh reflects the [McMahon Line](/wiki/McMahon_Line "McMahon Line"), a line demarcated by the 1914 [Simla Convention](/wiki/Simla_Convention "Simla Convention") between the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") and the [Tibetan](/wiki/Tibet_(1912%E2%80%931951) "Tibet (1912–1951)") government. The Simla Convention was never accepted by the Chinese government, and it was also considered invalid by Tibetans due to unmet conditions specified in the treaty.[[7]](#cite_note-Shakya1999-7) The boundary was not broadly enforced by the Indian government until 1950. Currently, this territory is administered by India.[[8]](#cite_note-Territorial_Disputes_and_Resource_Management:_A_Global_Handbook-8)[[9]](#cite_note-History_of_Soybeans_and_Soyfoods_in_South_Asia_/_Indian_Subcontinent_(1656-2010):_Extensively_Annotated_Bibliography_and_Sourcebook-9)

#### Republic of China's position

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Republic of China's position")]

Throughout the Cold War, the [Government of the Republic of China](/wiki/Government_of_the_Republic_of_China "Government of the Republic of China") had the same understanding of the China-India border dispute as the PRC.[[2]](#cite_note-book-2) In 1962, the [ROC's Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Taiwan) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Taiwan)") stated that they did not recognise the legality of the [McMahon Line](/wiki/McMahon_Line "McMahon Line"). The same year western countries increased pressure on the ROC leader [Chiang Kai-shek](/wiki/Chiang_Kai-shek "Chiang Kai-shek") to recognise the legality of the McMahon Line in order to isolate [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing").[[2]](#cite_note-book-2) However, Chiang dismissed the McMahon Line as an "imperialist imposition on China". In February 1987, India's move to elevate the status of "[Arunachal centrally administered region](/wiki/North-East_Frontier_Agency "North-East Frontier Agency")" to the state of [Arunachal Pradesh](/wiki/Arunachal_Pradesh "Arunachal Pradesh") was declared null and void by the ROC's [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Taiwan) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Taiwan)").[[2]](#cite_note-book-2) The Ministry, in a formal statement, stated that it did not recognise the "illegal occupation" of ROC territory south of the McMahon Line and that the establishment of "Arunachal Pradesh state" was an illegal act. In 1995, Ambassador [Teng Pei-Yin](/wiki/Teng_Pei-Yin "Teng Pei-Yin") (the ROC's first representative to India) in response to an Indian member of parliament, stated that the ROC did not recognise the McMahon Line.[[2]](#cite_note-book-2) However, the ROC has not made any statement on the China-India dispute since.[[2]](#cite_note-book-2)

#### Disputed areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Disputed areas")]

Main articles: [Disputed](/wiki/Disputed "Disputed"), [China](/wiki/China "China"), [India-China Border Roads](/wiki/India-China_Border_Roads "India-China Border Roads"), [Quadrilateral Security Dialogue](/wiki/Quadrilateral_Security_Dialogue "Quadrilateral Security Dialogue"), [QUAD](/wiki/QUAD "QUAD"), [Western Theater Command](/wiki/Western_Theater_Command "Western Theater Command"), [List of People's Liberation Army Air Force airbases](/wiki/List_of_People%27s_Liberation_Army_Air_Force_airbases "List of People's Liberation Army Air Force airbases"), and [List of Indian Air Force stations](/wiki/List_of_Indian_Air_Force_stations "List of Indian Air Force stations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Map_of_Western_Theatre_of_PLA.svg/250px-Map_of_Western_Theatre_of_PLA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Western_Theatre_of_PLA.svg)

China's integrated [Western Theater Command](/wiki/Western_Theater_Command "Western Theater Command") area.

The [Chinese Military](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") has an integrated [Western Theater Command](/wiki/Western_Theater_Command "Western Theater Command") across the entire border with India.[[10]](#cite_note-lac1-10) The [Indian Military](/wiki/Indian_Armed_Forces "Indian Armed Forces") has divided the Line of Actual Control into 3 sectors – the *western sector* across [Ladakh](/wiki/Ladakh "Ladakh") and the Chinese-held Aksai Chin, the *central sector* across [Himachal Pradesh](/wiki/Himachal_Pradesh "Himachal Pradesh") and [Uttarakhand](/wiki/Uttarakhand "Uttarakhand"), and the *eastern sector* across [Sikkim](/wiki/Sikkim "Sikkim") and Arunachal Pradesh.[[11]](#cite_note-lac2-11) Similarly, the [Indian Airforce](/wiki/Indian_Airforce "Indian Airforce") has a [New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi")-based [Western Air Command](/wiki/Western_Air_Command "Western Air Command"), a [Prayagraj](/wiki/Prayagraj "Prayagraj")-based [Central Air Command](/wiki/Central_Air_Command_(India) "Central Air Command (India)"), and a [Shillong](/wiki/Shillong "Shillong")-based [Eastern Air Command](/wiki/Eastern_Air_Command_(India) "Eastern Air Command (India)") with several AFS (Air Force Stations/Bases), AGL (Advanced Landing Ground airports) and helipads to cover the LAC.

Within the geostrategic context of disputes between India and China, the disputes over [Doklam](/wiki/Doklam "Doklam") and the [Ten Degree Channel](/wiki/Ten_Degree_Channel "Ten Degree Channel") and [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea") shipping lanes are also relevant. Doklam is a dispute between Bhutan and China near the India-Bhutan-China tri-junction. India has sided with Bhutan in the dispute.[[20]](#cite_note-Banyan-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Natl_Assembly-21) The area is of strategic importance to all three countries.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Doklam lies close to India's [Siliguri "Chickenneck" Corridor](/wiki/Siliguri_Corridor "Siliguri Corridor") where a Chinese military advance of less than 130 kilometres (81 mi) would cut off Bhutan, part of [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal") and all of [Northeast India](/wiki/Northeast_India "Northeast India"), an area containing almost 50 million people. This scenario arose during the war between India and China in 1962.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[18]](#cite_note-lac3-18) India has embarked on a slew of [Northeastern India connectivity](/wiki/Ministry_of_Development_of_North_Eastern_Region#Northeast_India_connectivity_projects "Ministry of Development of North Eastern Region") projects to create multiple alternatives to the Silliguri corridor, including through Bangladesh, National Waterways and seaports, to mitigate the risk of being cut off.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The world's busiest [Sea line of communication](/wiki/Sea_lines_of_communication "Sea lines of communication") (SLOC) passes through the [Strait of Malacca](/wiki/Strait_of_Malacca "Strait of Malacca") and the [Ten Degree Channel](/wiki/Ten_Degree_Channel "Ten Degree Channel") in the [exclusive economic zone of India](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone_of_India "Exclusive economic zone of India"), a region protected by India's [Andaman and Nicobar Command](/wiki/Andaman_and_Nicobar_Command "Andaman and Nicobar Command"). It is a geostrategic [choke point](/wiki/Choke_point "Choke point") for China since over 94,000 merchant ships travel through every year, carrying 40% of the world's freight trade between China and other nations.[[24]](#cite_note-stra2-24) US$5 trillion in annual shipping trade passes through the SLOC and choke points of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and the South China Sea (SCS).[[25]](#cite_note-stra3-25)[[26]](#cite_note-stra12-26) 80% of global trade passes through the Indian Ocean SLOC in the form of oil and natural gas critical for advanced economies.[[26]](#cite_note-stra12-26) India, [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") have comprised the [Quadrilateral Security Dialogue](/wiki/Quadrilateral_Security_Dialogue "Quadrilateral Security Dialogue") (QUAD) to contain China as part of their [Indo-Pacific](/wiki/Indo-Pacific "Indo-Pacific") strategy.[[27]](#cite_note-:0-27)[[28]](#cite_note-Chellaney-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-Tanning-30)

### Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Pakistan")]

Main article: [India–Pakistan border](/wiki/India%E2%80%93Pakistan_border "India–Pakistan border")

#### Kashmir

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Kashmir")]

Main article: [Kashmir conflict](/wiki/Kashmir_conflict "Kashmir conflict")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Kashmir_map.svg/250px-Kashmir_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kashmir_map.svg)

The divisions of the disputed [Kashmir region](/wiki/Kashmir_region "Kashmir region") administered by the republics of Pakistan, India, and China.

The conflict over [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir") is primarily between India and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), with China largely playing a third-party role.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) Conflict started after the [partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") in 1947, as both India and Pakistan initially claimed the entirety of the former princely state of [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(princely_state) "Jammu and Kashmir (princely state)"). In 1963, Pakistan [recognized Chinese sovereignty](/wiki/Sino-Pakistan_Agreement "Sino-Pakistan Agreement") over the [Trans-Karakoram Tract](/wiki/Trans-Karakoram_Tract "Trans-Karakoram Tract") and Aksai Chin.[[14]](#cite_note-Time-14) India controls 70% of Kashmir's population and approximately 55% of its land area including [Jammu](/wiki/Jammu "Jammu"), [Kashmir Valley](/wiki/Kashmir_Valley "Kashmir Valley"), most of Ladakh, and [Siachen Glacier](/wiki/Siachen_Glacier "Siachen Glacier").[[33]](#cite_note-Kargil_from_Surprise_to_Victory-33) Pakistan controls approximately 30% of the land including [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir") and [Gilgit-Baltistan](/wiki/Gilgit-Baltistan "Gilgit-Baltistan"). China controls the remaining 15% of the land, including Aksai Chin and the mostly uninhabited Trans-Karakoram Tract, as well as part of the [Demchok sector](/wiki/Demchok_sector "Demchok sector").[[34]](#cite_note-britannica-intro-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-War_Top_of_the_World-39)

#### Sir Creek

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Sir Creek")]

[Sir Creek](/wiki/Sir_Creek "Sir Creek") is a 96 km (60 mi) [tidal estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary") in the uninhabited marshlands of the [Indus River Delta](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") on the border between India and Pakistan. It flows into the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea") and separates [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat "Gujarat") state in [India](/wiki/India "India") from [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh") province in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan").[[40]](#cite_note-refute1-40) The long-standing India-Pakistan Sir Creek border dispute stems from the demarcation "from the mouth of Sir Creek to the top of Sir Creek, and from the top of Sir Creek eastward to a point on the line designated on the Western Terminus".[[40]](#cite_note-refute1-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) From this point onward, the boundary is unambiguously fixed as defined by the Tribunal Award of 1968.[[42]](#cite_note-tribune-42)

#### Junagadh and Manavadar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Junagadh and Manavadar")]

In a new political map released in 2020, Pakistan claimed the former princely states of [Junagadh](/wiki/Junagadh_State "Junagadh State") and [Manavadar](/wiki/Bantva_Manavadar "Bantva Manavadar").[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

### Nepal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Nepal")]

Main article: [India–Nepal border](/wiki/India%E2%80%93Nepal_border "India–Nepal border")

Major areas of dispute between India and Nepal are [Kalapani](/wiki/Kalapani_territory "Kalapani territory"), Limpiyadhura, Lipulekh, [Susta](/wiki/Susta_territory "Susta territory"), Mechi, and Tanakpur.[[46]](#cite_note-diplo-46) An estimated 60,000 hectares of border land is currently disputed between India and Nepal.[[47]](#cite_note-scroll-47)

#### Kalapani territory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Kalapani territory")]

Although claimed by Nepal, [Kalapani](/wiki/Kalapani_territory "Kalapani territory") has been administered by the Indian army since the [1962 border war with China](/wiki/Sino-Indian_War "Sino-Indian War") and Indian maps have been showing Kalapani as part of India since then.[[46]](#cite_note-diplo-46) The 1816 [Treaty of Sugauli](/wiki/Treaty_of_Sugauli "Treaty of Sugauli") signed by [Kingdom of Nepal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Nepal "Kingdom of Nepal") and [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") in 1816 utilizes the [Kali River](/wiki/Sharda_River "Sharda River") as Nepal's western boundary with India. However, lack of consensus on what is the precise location of the river Kali has resulted in dispute over whether the land consisting of Kalapani, [Limpiyadhura](/wiki/Kuthi_Valley "Kuthi Valley") and [Lipulekh](/wiki/Lipulekh_Pass "Lipulekh Pass") is part of India or Nepal.[[48]](#cite_note-express-48) Some scholars suggest that the lack of consensus is because of British cartographers who kept on shifting the line demarcating the river eastwards for strategic reasons. However, there are also some scholars who believe that the lack of consensus is due to the shift in the course of the river over time.[[48]](#cite_note-express-48)

In November 2019, India issued a new political map of the country which, like earlier maps, showed Kalapani as part of India. This new map was rejected by Nepal, and mass protests against India took place across the country and outside the Indian embassy in Nepal. Protesters accused India of occupying their land.[[49]](#cite_note-49) In May 2020, the Indian Defence minister, [Rajnath Singh](/wiki/Rajnath_Singh "Rajnath Singh") inaugurated an 80 km road from [Dharchula](/wiki/Dharchula "Dharchula") to Lipulekh pass. Nepal protested against the construction of the road.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The Lipulekh area is currently under India's control. On 20 May 2020, Nepal launched its own map showing Kalapani, Limpiyadhura and Lipulekh as parts of its own territory.[[51]](#cite_note-51) The new map was subsequently approved by the [Nepali parliament](/wiki/Federal_Parliament_of_Nepal "Federal Parliament of Nepal") on 18 June 2020. India opposed the move, claiming that the decision to include Limpiyadhura, Lipulekh and Kalapani is not based on evidence or historical facts.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

On 15 June 2020, the Indian border police, [Sashastra Seema Bal](/wiki/Sashastra_Seema_Bal "Sashastra Seema Bal") (SSB), reported that [border pillars](/wiki/Boundary_marker "Boundary marker"), which served as demarcation, were missing along the India-Nepal border. SSB also reported that Nepali border police had established five new [border outposts](/wiki/Border_outpost "Border outpost") near the disputed area.[[53]](#cite_note-53) On 19 June, Nepal started deploying troops near the disputed areas. The troops had established camps and they were working on building a [helipad](/wiki/Helipad "Helipad") in the area.[[54]](#cite_note-54) In the same month Nepali Army Chief, General [Purna Chandra Thapa](/wiki/Purna_Chandra_Thapa "Purna Chandra Thapa"), after visiting the Kalapani border area, stated that the [Nepali Army](/wiki/Nepali_Army "Nepali Army") will start building army barracks and border outposts near the Kalapani area.[[55]](#cite_note-55) On 21 June, Indian residents in border villages of Dharchula and Jhoolaghat in [Pithoragarh district](/wiki/Pithoragarh_district "Pithoragarh district") claimed that radio stations in Nepal were conducting a propaganda campaign against India by playing anti-India songs and biased news bulletins showing India in a bad light.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

#### Susta territory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Susta territory")]

[Susta](/wiki/Susta_territory "Susta territory") is another territory that is disputed between Nepal and India. It is currently controlled by India as a part of the [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") state. [[57]](#cite_note-susta-57) The 1816 [Treaty of Sugauli](/wiki/Treaty_of_Sugauli "Treaty of Sugauli") defined [Gandaki River](/wiki/Gandaki_River "Gandaki River") as the international boundary between India and Nepal. The right bank of the river was under Nepal's control while the left bank was under India's control. Susta village was initially on the right bank when the treaty was signed and it was a part of Nepal. However, over the years, the Gandaki River changed its course and Susta moved to the left bank and is now under India's control.[[57]](#cite_note-susta-57) The [Government of Nepal](/wiki/Government_of_Nepal "Government of Nepal") has repeatedly stated that Susta belongs to Nepal and that the Indian government should return the area.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Resolved disputes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Resolved disputes")]

### Sri Lanka

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sri Lanka")]

Main article: [India–Sri Lanka maritime boundary agreements](/wiki/India%E2%80%93Sri_Lanka_maritime_boundary_agreements "India–Sri Lanka maritime boundary agreements")

The dispute on the status of the island of [Katchatheevu](/wiki/Katchatheevu "Katchatheevu") was settled in 1974 by an agreement between both countries.[[58]](#cite_note-58) But still some cases are ongoing in [High Court of Madras](/wiki/Madras_High_Court "Madras High Court") which are filed earlier regarding this dispute stating as illegally issued to [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka").[[59]](#cite_note-59) Several actions were still taken by the [Union Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") to retrieve that island back to Indian territory once again.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Bangladesh

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_disputes_of_India&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Bangladesh")]

Main articles: [Bangladesh–India border](/wiki/Bangladesh%E2%80%93India_border "Bangladesh–India border") and [India–Bangladesh enclaves](/wiki/India%E2%80%93Bangladesh_enclaves "India–Bangladesh enclaves")

[South Talpatti](/wiki/South_Talpatti "South Talpatti") (as it was known internationally) or Sheikh Mujib Island (as it was known by Bangladeshi [Awami League](/wiki/Awami_League "Awami League") supporters) was a small uninhabited offshore sandbar landform in the [Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Bay_of_Bengal "Bay of Bengal"), off the coast of the [Ganga-Brahmaputra Delta](/wiki/Ganges_Delta "Ganges Delta") region. In the aftermath of the [Bhola cyclone](/wiki/Bhola_cyclone "Bhola cyclone") in 1970, it emerged in the Bay of Bengal on the [Bangladeshi](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") side of the dividing river, and disappeared before or during [Cyclone Aila](/wiki/Cyclone_Aila "Cyclone Aila") in 2009. In March 2010, Sugata Hazra of the School of Oceanographic Studies at [Jadavpur University](/wiki/Jadavpur_University "Jadavpur University"), [Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata"), said that the island had disappeared and that [sea level rise](/wiki/Current_sea_level_rise "Current sea level rise") caused by climate change was a factor.[[61]](#cite_note-Wade-61)

The enclaves were reputedly part of a high stake card or chess games centuries ago between two regional kings, the Maharaja of [Cooch Behar](/wiki/Cooch_Behar "Cooch Behar") and the Nawab of [Rangpur](/wiki/Rangpur,_Bangladesh "Rangpur, Bangladesh") and the result of a confused outcome of a treaty between the Kingdom of Cooch Behar and the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"). After the [partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") in 1947, [Cooch Behar district](/wiki/Cooch_Behar_district "Cooch Behar district") joined India and Rangpur went to [East Pakistan](/wiki/East_Pakistan "East Pakistan"), which became Bangladesh in 1971. The prime ministers of India and Bangladesh signed a Land Boundary Agreement in 1974 to exchange enclaves and simplify the international border. In 1974, Bangladesh approved a proposed treaty to exchange all enclaves within each other's territories, but India did not ratify it. Another agreement was made in 2011 to exchange enclaves and adverse possessions. A revised version of the agreement was finally adopted by the two countries 41 years after negotiations began, when the Parliament of India passed the 100th Amendment Act to the Indian Constitution on 7 May 2015.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Inside the main part of [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), there were 111 Indian enclaves (17,160.63 acres), while in the main part of India, there were 51 Bangladeshi enclaves (7,110.02 acres). In respect of adverse possessions, India received 2,777.038 acres of land and transferred 2267.682 acres to Bangladesh. India ratified the agreement by constitutional amendment in May 2015.[[63]](#cite_note-India_2014-63) Under this agreement, the enclave residents could continue to reside at their present location or move to the country of their choice.[[64]](#cite_note-Sougata_Mukhopadhyay-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) The [adverse possession](/wiki/Adverse_possession "Adverse possession") of Berubari went to Bangladesh.[[66]](#cite_note-66) The unmarked borders between the nations were also finally solved regarding Daikhata-Dumabari, Muhurichar river island[[67]](#cite_note-Shubhajit_Roy-67) and Pyrdiwah.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

In 2009, Bangladesh initiated arbitration proceedings against India under the [United Nations Convention on Law of Sea](/wiki/United_Nations_Convention_on_the_Law_of_the_Sea "United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea"). In 2014 it was awarded a large part of the Bay of Bengal, ending the maritime dispute between the two countries.[[69]](#cite_note-69)
